text: I find it fascinating that octopuses have distributed intelligence — about two-thirds of their neurons are in their arms rather than their central brain. This means each arm can essentially think and solve problems somewhat independently. An octopus can lose an arm and that arm will continue to react to stimuli for a while, almost as if it has a mind of its own. This raises genuinely strange questions about consciousness and what it means to have unified experience when your body is wired so differently from most animals. It suggests that intelligence and decision-making don't require the kind of centralized control we mammals assume is necessary. There's something both unsettling and beautiful about that — a creature whose very neurology challenges our assumptions about how minds must work.
